inline_comments.message: In theory, this is not necessary, see line 135.  It should be sourced in from that line (that would be expanded).  In general, .sh does not need expansion, provided the environment capture all values needed.

This should not be necessary if env.sh is populated correctly.   I think is best is to source in env.sh:

source $POLICY_HOME/etc/profile.d/env.sh

It should contain POLICY_LOGS.   Typically all the scripts should do this, if they don't.

Note also that elk.sh is not used in ONAP, there's a ticket opened in this area against Ravi in the backlog.

take a look at my other comments, in  other files.   For an installer is easier to understand absolute path locations vs. suffixes, ..   To me is better to preserve old variable names LOGPATH and PARSERLOGPATH and keep absolute path semantics, it reduces risks a little bit too.
See my other comments in other files, about perhaps keeping the same variable names.

I think since the logging area in PE is so shaky probably better to keep this just to catch processes that log into this directory, so we know.   Otherwise, if this directory does not exist, we may not catch them, or we may have some unpredictable behavior.   So, being conservative, given the time in the release where we are at, probably can't tolerate breakage.

Better, to create another mkdir that also create the POLICY_LOGS/policy directory to keep all bases safe.

I think for the time being, better to keep the same variables, otherwise we need to change them in the docker repo and oom repos.   Better to keep scope of changes small.   Simply expand the ${{POLICY_LOGS}} with the value from packages/install/src/files/base.conf.   The same change will apply to docker repo and oom repo.   These paths should be know a priori by the installer.

As an installer, it is easier to understand the absolute path where I want to store logs vs. having to understand suffixes, and what is the right prefix, so better to interact with absolute values.

The original locations were incorrect from the start.

LOGPATH=...../policy/pap/pap-rest.log

should be something like,

LOGPATH=...../policy/paplp/paplp.log

For:

PARSERLOGPATH=.../policy/pap/IntegrityMonitor.log

should be

PARSERLOGPATH=.../policy/paplp/paplp.log

The same, not sure why there are 2 for just a library, may be there is some problem underneath.  You know better Jim, if it will cause a problem to reuse the same file, give it another name but still under paplp component.
same comment about not changing variable names to reduce the scope of changes at this point of the release
Is this still necessary?
Ok.  Removed the extra braces.
Ok.  Removed the extra braces and dotted in env.sh
Ok
Ok.  Will keep both
Yes, per Jorge's previous comment
OK.  Will replace the variable with the expanded value.
I'm uncomfortable changing the file names at this point.  Will leave them as is.

Question Jim, POLICY_LOGS is known here, correct?   can we do "mkdir -p $POLICY_LOGS"?   In theory, POLICY_LOGS should be anywhere in the file system.    Typically developer installation won't install logs directory under /var/log/onap.   It requires priviledge access, and typically this script will run as non-root user, so will fail and exit.

The point I am trying to make is that /var/log is typically root owned.   Some developer scenarions will want to use the "policy" owned directory (or non-root directory).   In those scenarios, installation would abort, since not having root privilege, or will create directory unnecessarily.

I think here, it can be left as /opt/app/policy/logs, that's OK.   What is important is to modify the "docker" project, repo which is the one that customizes for ONAP.   I think for pdp-d we have like this, for any other installation that is not ONAP, it will install under $POLICY_HOME (which is kind of nice, keeping all together, without needing any root privileges).

The important thing that drives the ONAP installation, is the docker repo project, so that base.conf is the one that needs to be modified for onap installs.

I think here, it can be left as /opt/app/policy/logs ..  the important thing is to have them point to /var/log/onap/.. in the docker project.   

Leaving it as /opt/app/policy/logs won't interfere with other non-ONAP installations.   This may have side effects for non-ONAP installs.
same comment as in previous file with regards to leaving path under /opt/app/policy ..  The important thing for ONAP is what goes in the "docker" repo.
I think we should remove the exit 1, case /var/log/onap is not used for logging (installation dependent) (see comments on patch 4 for this one and other files)

Yes, changed to use $POLICY_LOGS.  I'm leaving the "exit 1" as that it won't cause an issue if the directory already exists, and it will abort, as seems appropriate, if the directory does not exist and cannot be created.

Unfortunately, there are still places where the path is hard-coded (e.g., log4j.properties).  Until those are updated, this must continue to point to /var/log/onap.  (The same holds true for the pdplp.conf & paplp.conf files.)  The only way to remove the hard coding from log4j.properties is to add $POLICY_LOGS to the JVM options, which I can do, but not sure we want to make that change at this point.
